summary: Performance, memory-footprint & UI-responsiveness audit of the FileID Windows app+engine (4GB/low-mem, tens-of-thousands of files)
logs: Perf audit: 12 raw; 7 CONFIRMED (>=2/3 real & safe); 1 contested

confirmed.title: applyTags writes a sidecar JSON + IPropertyStore COM call per file INSIDE the open writer transaction, holding the single-writer mutex for the whole bulk op
confirmed.category: oversized-transaction / per-row I-O under the writer lock
confirmed.description: handle_apply_tags opens one transaction (line 96) and loops over payload.file_ids (capped at MAX_FILES = 100_000, line 75). For every successfully-tagged file it (a) re-SELECTs all of that file's user tags (lines 152-156) and (b) calls crate::shell::tags::write_tags(path, &tags) (line 157) — which does a synchronous sidecar temp-file write + rename AND, on Windows, an IPropertyStore COM write (shell/tags.rs::write_tags -> write_sidecar + windows_ipropertystore::write_keywords). All of this happens while still inside the open transaction, and tx.commit() is not reached until after the entire loop (line 169). The connection is the engine's single Arc<Mutex<Connection>> writer, so the lock (conn = db.lock(), line 95) is held for the full duration. IPropertyStore COM writes are routinely 1-10 ms each; sidecar temp-write+rename is two filesystem ops each. The cost is therefore (file_count) x (2 fs ops + 1 COM call) serialized under the writer mutex within ONE uncommitted transaction.
confirmed.trigger: A 'tag all' / bulk-tag action over a large selection (tens of thousands of files; the handler permits up to 100K). On a 50K-file library that is ~50K COM calls + ~100K filesystem ops, blocking the engine's only writer for tens of seconds to minutes — any concurrent scan dbwriter flush stalls behind it, and the single uncommitted transaction grows the WAL with every tag/delete row until commit.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Do only the DB work inside the transaction. In the per-file loop, after the tag rows are written, push (path, tags) onto a Vec; commit the transaction (releasing the writer mutex); THEN iterate that Vec calling write_tags outside the lock. The sidecar/COM writes don't need transactional atomicity with the DB (write_tags failures are already only logged, line 159), so moving them past the commit preserves behavior while unblocking the writer and bounding WAL growth.

verdicts.reasoning:
The code mechanics are accurately described: handle_apply_tags (bulk.rs:95-169) holds the single-writer mutex (db.lock() line 95) across one uncommitted transaction (line 96) for the whole file_ids loop, and calls shell::tags::write_tags (line 157) inside it. write_tags (shell/tags.rs:52-64) does a sidecar temp-write+rename (2 fs ops) plus a per-file IPropertyStore COM round-trip (CoInitializeEx + SHGetPropertyStoreFromParsingName + SetValue + Commit + CoUninitialize, lines 195-215). The dbwriter shares the SAME Arc<Mutex<Connection>> (main.rs:166-168; dbwriter.rs:175 self.conn.lock()), so a held lock can genuinely stall scan flushes. The suggested fix (collect (path,tags) inside the tx, commit, then write_tags outside the lock) is behaviorally safe since write_tags failures are already only logged (line 159).

What refutes the HIGH severity is the scale. The finder's cost model depends entirely on tens-of-thousands-to-100K files ("a 50K-file library = ~50K COM calls"), but that scale is unreachable from the UI. The only bulk caller OnTagSelectedClicked (LibraryView.xaml.cs:978) draws ids from ViewModel.SelectedItems, a subset of ViewModel.Items. Items is ALWAYS replaced (ReplaceItems, LibraryViewModel.cs:434-455 — never appended) with a result hard-capped at PageSize=200 on every load path (RecentAsync/SearchAsync lines 292/296, SemanticSearchAsync 351, SimilarFilesAsync 391). There is no load-more/pagination growth and no "tag entire library" command; the other caller (FilePreviewSheet.xaml.cs:914) tags a single file. So realistic _fileIds.Count <= 200. The MAX_FILES=100_000 constant (line 75) is an explicitly-documented defensive guard against a buggy IPC peer, not an operating scale — the finder mistook the guard ceiling for the workload.

Re-derived cost: ~200 files x (1-10 ms COM + 2 fs ops) ~= 200 ms-2 s of writer-lock hold for an explicit, infrequent user action (not a per-file/per-frame/per-scan-event hot path), comfortably under the app's own 30 s IPC timeout (BulkTagSheet.xaml.cs:76). WAL growth from <=200 tag rows is negligible. The fix is sound, cheap, and worth doing as hygiene, but the claimed "high / tens of seconds to minutes / 50K COM calls" impact rests on a scale the real callers cannot produce. Off the genuine critical path at realistic scale -> refuted. Key files: platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/bulk.rs, platforms/windows/src/engine/src/shell/tags.rs, platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/ViewModels/LibraryViewModel.cs, platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/Views/Library/LibraryView.xaml.cs, platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/Views/Library/BulkTagSheet.xaml.cs.

VERIFIED IN CODE. handle_apply_tags (platforms/windows/src/engine/src/commands/bulk.rs:64) takes conn = db.lock() (line 95), opens one transaction (line 96), and the per-file loop (lines 101-168) is not committed until line 169 — so the engine's single writer mutex is held for the whole bulk op. The Arc is the only writer: dbwriter.rs:175 (flush) locks the same self.conn, and main.rs:166/585 clone the single db_conn Arc into the handler, so the claim "concurrent scan dbwriter flush stalls behind it" is correct.

PER-FILE WORK INSIDE THE LOCK (for each succeeded file, lines 151-167): (a) a tag re-SELECT (cheap), (b) crate::shell::tags::write_tags (line 157). write_tags (shell/tags.rs:52-64) does write_sidecar (two filesystem ops: std::fs::write temp + std::fs::rename, lines 79-81) AND on Windows windows_ipropertystore::write_keywords. write_keywords (tags.rs:195-215) per call: CoInitializeEx+CoUninitialize (with_com, lines 183-193), SHGetPropertyStoreFromParsingName (line 173 — instantiates the registered per-extension shell property-handler COM object, cold-path DLL load + file parse), then SetValue + Commit (lines 210-212), where Commit forces an embedded-keyword write into the actual file. This is the dominant cost: IPropertyStore SetValue+Commit through a real handler is routinely 1-10 ms/file (higher on first handler load); the sidecar adds two fs ops (heavier on the NAS corpus). Realistic ~2-12 ms/file.

MAGNITUDE AT SCALE: 10K files ≈ 20-120 s of held writer lock; 50K files ≈ 100-600 s — matching the "tens of seconds to minutes" estimate, and well past the app's 30 s WaitForBulkActionResultAsync timeout (BulkTagSheet.xaml.cs:76). It's user-initiated and off the scan throughput path, but the harm is concrete: (1) the only DB writer is wedged for that whole window so any in-flight scan batch flush blocks, and (2) every tag row stays in one uncommitted transaction, growing the WAL until commit — a real concern on the 4 GB target. The handler explicitly permits up to MAX_FILES = 100_000 (line 75), so the worst case is reachable by design, not theoretical.

FIX IS SAFE: write_tags failures are already only logged and never touch the DB row or the succeeded count (bulk.rs:158-160). There is no transactional coupling between the DB tag rows and the sidecar/COM write, so moving (path, tags) collection inside the tx, committing, then iterating write_tags outside the lock preserves observable behavior exactly while releasing the writer and bounding WAL growth. No correctness/quality regression.

Confidence held at 0.82 (not higher) because the exact per-file IPropertyStore cost is hardware/handler/extension-dependent and not measured on the dev box here; but even at the low end (~2 ms/file) the lock-hold at the documented scales is clearly significant, so the verdict does not hinge on the upper estimate.

VERIFIED against source. handle_apply_tags (bulk.rs:64-180) opens ONE transaction on the engine's single writer connection (conn = db.lock(), line 95; tx at 96) and does NOT commit until line 169, after looping over up to MAX_FILES=100_000 file_ids (line 75). Inside the loop, for every successfully-tagged file it calls crate::shell::tags::write_tags (line 157) while still holding the lock and inside the open tx.

Per-file cost of write_tags is the real bottleneck, NOT the DB work. tags.rs::write_tags -> write_sidecar (std::fs::write temp + std::fs::rename = 2 metadata-touching syscalls, lines 79-82) AND windows_ipropertystore::write_keywords (lines 195-215), which runs a FULL per-file COM cycle: CoInitializeEx -> SHGetPropertyStoreFromParsingName -> SetValue -> Commit -> CoUninitialize (with_com, lines 183-193). IPropertyStore::Commit invokes the shell property handler — routinely 1-10 ms, worse on network/RAW/Office handlers. By contrast the in-tx DB work is plain INSERT/DELETE on the `tags` table with prepare_cached statements and NO FTS triggers (confirmed: only ocr_fts/doc_fts exist, migrations.rs), i.e. sub-microsecond per row. So the lock is held for (file_count) x (2 fs ops + 1 full COM init/commit/uninit), dominated by COM.

Scale: the dev/test corpus is G:\TrueNAS (network storage) where both fs and COM costs spike. A 50K-file bulk tag at a conservative 2-5 ms/file blended = ~100-250 s, entirely under the single Arc<parking_lot::Mutex<Connection>> writer (main.rs:166-168, shared by every handler). Confirmed contender: the scan dbwriter locks the SAME mutex for its whole batch commit (dbwriter.rs:175) and would stall behind this; so would face_clustering/restructure/trash/rename. Corroborating the severity, the app's own WaitForBulkActionResultAsync waits only 30 s (BulkTagSheet.xaml.cs:76) — a large bulk tag blows past it.

FIX IS SAFE (regression-risk lens): the tags are already materialized inside the tx (SELECT-back at lines 152-156) BEFORE write_tags is called, so capturing (path, tags) into a Vec and running write_tags after tx.commit() writes byte-identical sidecar/IPropertyStore content (same path, same tag vector, same order). write_tags failures are already log-only (line 159) and are NOT counted toward succeeded/failed (succeeded++ at line 161 fires regardless), so moving them post-commit leaves the BulkActionResult counts unchanged. No ML/embedding/clustering/face output is touched — these are pure write-only side-effects with no in-op read-back, so the gated 6 GB-box byte-identical behavior is unaffected (this is off the scan/ML path entirely). The fix is in fact strictly safer on DB-commit failure: today sidecars are written before commit and would be orphaned against a rolled-back DB; post-commit they only land after a durable commit.

Net: genuinely significant at the target scale AND the fix preserves correctness and quality. Confidence held below 0.9 only because the exact COM/fs per-file latency on the user's specific storage needs an on-hardware A/B to pin the absolute seconds; the relative win (releasing the writer mutex + bounding the uncommitted tx) is unambiguous from the code.

confirmed.title: Face-crop JPEG encode + dir-create + disk write run synchronously inside the SQLite writer transaction (blocks the async DBWriter task and the single writer lock per face)
confirmed.description: In flush(), the connection mutex is taken once for the whole batch (let conn = self.conn.lock(); at line 175) and a transaction is opened. Inside the per-file loop, for every detected face save_face_crop(face_id, crop) (line 388) is called WHILE that lock + open transaction are held. save_face_crop (line 713) does, per face: crate::paths::faces_dir(), std::fs::create_dir_all(&dir) (a filesystem syscall on EVERY face even though the dir already exists after the first), crop_rgb_112.to_vec() (a 37 KB copy), a full JPEG encode (image::DynamicImage::write_to), and std::fs::write to disk. All of this is synchronous blocking work executed on the async DBWriter task (writer.run(...).await in scan_session.rs:351) and serialized inside the global single-writer critical section. At scale this is the throughput cliff the lens targets: a 10k-30k photo library with even 1-2 faces/photo performs 10k-60k synchronous JPEG encodes + create_dir_all syscalls + disk writes, each one (a) parking a tokio runtime worker on blocking disk I/O and (b) holding the one writer connection lock so no other DB progress (and ultimately the whole tagging->dbwriter backpressure chain) can advance. Disk-write latency (AV scanner, spinning disk, network profile dir) directly stalls the pipeline that should be GPU-bound. The deferred 'dbwriter 512-byte per-face embedding clone' is a different, smaller item; this is the dominant per-face cost.
confirmed.trigger: Scan a face-heavy library (tens of thousands of photos, multiple faces each) on the 4 GB box; each batch flush holds conn.lock() across N synchronous JPEG encodes + create_dir_all + fs::write.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Do not encode/write crops inside the transaction or under conn.lock(). Move crop bytes (face_id, rgb) out of the lock and persist them after tx.commit() — ideally hand the (id, crop) list to a dedicated spawn_blocking task (or a small bounded crop-writer thread/channel) so JPEG encode + fs::write never block the async writer or the DB lock. Call faces_dir()/create_dir_all once per scan (cache the dir, drop the per-face create_dir_all). Avoid crop_rgb_112.to_vec(): encode from the borrowed slice via ImageBuffer::<_, &[u8]>::from_raw.

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against the real code. save_face_crop (dbwriter.rs:713-728) is called at dbwriter.rs:388 for EVERY detected face (crop is always populated for embedded faces — tagging.rs:1410), and it runs inside both the open write transaction (tx opened at :176, committed at :488) and under conn.lock() (:175, dropped at :523). Per face it does create_dir_all (a stat syscall on every face), crop_rgb_112.to_vec() (37 KB copy), a full pure-Rust JPEG encode (image 0.25 baseline, Cargo.toml:81), and fs::write. The flush is awaited INLINE on the scan future (scan_session.rs:351, writer.run(...).await — not spawn_blocking), and the Tagging->DBWriter channel is bounded at 256 (tagging.rs:121, whose own comment warns a "slow flush" stalls workers). So serialized crop I/O lengthens each batch flush, backs up the bounded channel, and stalls the GPU-bound inference workers on send().await — real backpressure on the per-file critical path.

Quantified: per batch (~250 files, ~1.5 faces each = ~375 crops) the encode+write is ~0.1-0.4 s on a warm local SSD but ~0.4-2 s under Defender on-write scanning / spinning disk / network profile dir (the explicitly named trigger; every new JPEG triggers an AV scan). Across a 10k-30k library that is 15k-60k synchronous encodes+writes — ~7-30 s of added flush-blocking warm, 30-90+ s under AV, interleaved as recurring stalls of the pipeline that should be GPU-bound.

One sub-claim is overstated: "no other DB progress can advance / holding the one writer lock." Reads use ephemeral read-only WAL connections (db/mod.rs:1-7,70-78) and never take this mutex, and during a scan the DbWriter is the sole writer — so the lock-contention framing is essentially nil. The real harm is the in-transaction blocking I/O on the inline-awaited flush task feeding a bounded channel, which the description also states correctly.

Fix is safe and quality-neutral: crop JPEGs are explicitly cosmetic (dbwriter.rs:730-732), and the codebase already establishes that crop file I/O belongs after commit (prune-after-commit at :490-494). Moving (face_id, crop) out to a spawn_blocking/bounded crop-writer after tx.commit(), caching faces_dir()/create_dir_all once per scan, and encoding from a borrowed slice (ImageBuffer<_, &[u8]>::from_raw) introduce no correctness or quality regression. face_id must be captured pre-commit via last_insert_rowid() — the suggested (id, crop) collection already handles this. Confidence held below 0.9 because the absolute warm-SSD cost depends on real disk/AV latency (an on-hardware A/B would pin the exact magnitude), but the mechanism, scale, and fix-safety are solid. Relevant file: C:/Users/adamm/Desktop/Code/FileID/platforms/windows/src/engine/src/pipeline/dbwriter.rs.

The code mechanics in the claim are accurate (dbwriter.rs:175 takes conn.lock() for the whole batch; the per-file loop calls save_face_crop at line 388 inside the open tx; save_face_crop at 713 does faces_dir()+create_dir_all+to_vec()+JPEG encode+fs::write per face). But the magnitude and the contention attribution are both overstated.

QUANTIFIED COST: A 112x112 RGB JPEG encode via image 0.25's pure-Rust encoder is ~0.2-0.5 ms warm (~0.3 ms typical). to_vec() of 37,632 bytes is ~3-5 us; create_dir_all on an existing dir is one cached stat (~1-10 us warm); fs::write of ~3-5 KB to OS cache is ~10-50 us. Dominant per-face cost ~0.3 ms. Aggregate for a 30k-photo, 1.5-face library: ~45k encodes x 0.3 ms ~= 14 s of CPU spread across a MULTI-HOUR scan, overlappable across the multi-thread runtime's other workers.

WHY NOT A CLIFF:
1) The writer lock is UNCONTENDED during steady-state scan. Verified: the GPU-bound tagger never takes db_conn.lock() (it locks the model mutex arcface_mu at tagging.rs:1396, not the DB); reads fan out via fresh read-only connections (CLAUDE.md invariant); the only other db_conn.lock() sites (scan_session.rs:144/165 setup, :374 completion stamp) run before/after writer.run().await, never concurrently. So holding the lock longer blocks no other DB progress — the claim's "no other DB progress can advance / whole backpressure chain stalls" is false for the scan path.
2) Flush is ~0.3-0.6% of the production window. On the 4 GB DirectML box, producing one 250-file batch costs ~50 s of GPU inference (RAM++ Swin-L@384 + CLIP + YuNet + per-face SFace); the entire flush incl. crops is ~150-300 ms. The per-face SFace embed that produced each crop (~5-30 ms GPU) already dwarfs the 0.3 ms encode by 1-2 orders of magnitude. Pipeline is GPU-bound by ~100x.
3) The tagging->dbwriter channel has 256 files of slack (TAGGING_CHANNEL_CAP=256, tagging.rs:121), absorbing bursty flush latency before it can stall the tagger.

The only legitimate concern is a TAIL: if %LOCALAPPDATA% is on an AV-scanned/spinning/network-redirected volume, serialized fs::write/create_dir_all per face can spike — but it would take pathological sustained multi-second-per-batch latency to backpressure a ~50 s/batch GPU pipeline, and the uncontended lock means it still blocks no other DB work. The suggested fixes (cache faces_dir, drop per-face create_dir_all, encode from a borrowed slice, optional spawn_blocking) are individually safe micro-optimizations but do not address a real throughput bottleneck at the target scale. Confidence is held below 0.9 because the disk-tail scenario on adverse storage is real and not measured on-hardware; flush is non-trivially fattened by crops but well within the GPU-bound budget.

CONFIRMED in code. dbwriter.rs:175 takes the single-writer conn lock once per batch; :176 opens a transaction; the per-file loop (:240) calls save_face_crop (:388) for every detected face while BOTH the lock and the open tx are held; tx.commit() is at :488. The writer runs as writer.run(...).await directly on a multi-threaded tokio runtime (main.rs:69 new_multi_thread; scan_session.rs:351 — no spawn_blocking wrapper), so each synchronous unit parks a shared runtime worker.

Per-face cost in save_face_crop (dbwriter.rs:713-728), all synchronous and blocking: faces_dir() string joins; std::fs::create_dir_all (a metadata syscall on EVERY face, redundant after launch — paths::ensure_state_dirs() at main.rs:83 already creates face_crops/ via paths.rs:64-71); crop_rgb_112.to_vec() (a 37632-byte copy, 112*112*3 confirmed at tagging.rs:2116 / sface.rs:75); a full baseline JPEG encode of the 112x112 image (image::DynamicImage::write_to); and std::fs::write (open+write+close). Typical ~0.3-1 ms/face warm, with a fat right tail under AV scanner / spinning / network-profile disk. For the stated face-heavy 10k-30k library at 1-2 faces/photo (10k-60k faces) that is order ~3-60 s of work executed serially inside the writer critical section — so no other DB progress advances and the GPU-bound pipeline backpressures on disk latency. Mechanism and scale are as described and on the critical path.

REGRESSION-RISK LENS — the fix is SAFE. The crop JPEG is a pure UI artifact: consumed only by face_crop_path() (cluster_suggestions.rs:137, VLM pair-verify input) and People-tab card rendering. Face clustering/identity use the arcface_embedding BLOB written by INSERT_FACE_SQL (dbwriter.rs:693), never the on-disk JPEG. Moving encode/write out of the lock and after tx.commit() (or to a dedicated spawn_blocking/bounded crop-writer channel, as suggested), dropping the per-face create_dir_all, and encoding from a borrowed slice via ImageBuffer::from_raw all yield byte-identical JPEGs and identical DB content — no change to embeddings, tags, clustering, or the gated 6 GB-box DB behavior (DB writes are untouched). The existing post-commit crop-prune (dbwriter.rs:490-494) already establishes that crop side effects belong outside the tx, so the fix is consistent with current invariants.

Caveat lowering confidence from certainty: the absolute throughput win depends on real per-face disk latency vs. how GPU-bound the pipeline actually is on the 4 GB box (an on-hardware A/B would pin the number), and a half-measure that only moves the work after commit but keeps it in the writer task still parks the worker — only the spawn_blocking/dedicated-thread form (which the suggestion correctly specifies) removes both the lock-hold and the worker-parking. But the cost is significant at the target scale and the suggested fix is correctness- and quality-safe, so this is real.

confirmed.title: DebugLog writes synchronously to disk (open+stat+append+close, under a global lock) on every IPC event from the UI thread
confirmed.description: DebugLog.Write (lines 55-83) does, for EVERY single log call, with no level gating and no buffering: AppPaths.EnsureDirectories(), File.Exists(path) + new FileInfo(path).Length (two filesystem stat syscalls), then File.AppendAllText (lines 63-75) which OPENS, writes, flushes, and CLOSES the log file handle every time, all serialized under a single process-global lock (s_writeLock). EngineClient.Apply runs ON THE UI THREAD (StdoutLoopAsync dispatches every decoded frame via _ui.TryEnqueue(() => Apply(ev)), EngineClient.cs:705) and unconditionally emits TWO Info writes per event ('[APPLY:N] enter' at EngineClient.cs:949 and '[APPLY:N] exit' at EngineClient.cs:1260). On top of that, each EngineClient.PropertyChanged fanout (Set<T>, EngineClient.cs:1294) synchronously invokes ~15 subscriber handlers on the same UI thread, several of which add a '[ENGINE-SUB:...]' Debug write (e.g. SidebarPipelineProgress.xaml.cs:179 fires on every LastProgress). During a scan the engine throttles Progress to 10 Hz (scan_session.rs PROGRESS_THROTTLE_MS=100) plus ~1.4 BatchSummary/s at 140 files/s, so ~12 events/s reach Apply. Cost mechanism: ~12 events/s x (2 Apply Info + >=1 ENGINE-SUB Debug) = ~30+ full open/stat/append/close cycles per second, each blocking the UI thread and serialized on one global lock. SCALE: a 'tens of thousands of files' scan keeps this rate up for the entire multi-minute scan; on the 4 GB low-memory target the disk is simultaneously contended by the engine's SQLite WAL writes (and may be an HDD or a paging-pressured SSD), so each AppendAllText open/flush can stall multiple ms — directly stealing time from grid layout/composition and producing the exact mid-scan UI stutter the throttles elsewhere were added to avoid. The 10 MB truncation FileInfo check also re-stats on every call.
confirmed.trigger: Read DebugLog.Write (open/stat/append/close per call under a global lock, no buffering, no level gate) and traced its hottest caller: EngineClient.Apply runs on the UI thread (EngineClient.cs:705 _ui.TryEnqueue) and logs unconditionally twice per IPC event (lines 949, 1260); confirmed engine emits ~10 Hz Progress + per-100-file BatchSummary (scan_session.rs:529,504) so the write rate tracks scan throughput, and ~15 PropertyChanged subscribers add more ENGINE-SUB Debug writes per event.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Do NOT strip the [APPLY:N]/[ENGINE-SUB] tracing (CLAUDE.md marks it load-bearing for fast-fail forensics). Instead make the SINK cheap and off-thread: keep an open StreamWriter (or buffer lines in a ConcurrentQueue / Channel) and flush on a dedicated background thread/timer, so individual Info/Debug calls become a near-free enqueue instead of an open-stat-append-close-flush cycle. At minimum, hold one persistent FileStream open (AppendAllText reopens every call) and move the >10 MB size check to a once-per-N-writes or startup-only check rather than a File.Exists+FileInfo stat on every line. This removes the per-event UI-thread disk I/O without changing what gets logged.

verdicts.reasoning:
VERIFIED REAL. Re-derived the cost from the actual code.

CORE CLAIM HOLDS: DebugLog.Write (Services/DebugLog.cs:55-83) does, per call, with no level gate and no buffering: File.Exists (1 stat, line 63) + new FileInfo(path).Length (1 stat EVERY call, line 65) + File.AppendAllText (open+write+flush+close, line 75), all serialized on the process-global s_writeLock (line 73). DebugLog.Debug has NO [Conditional("DEBUG")] attribute, so these run in RELEASE builds too — confirmed by grep (no #if DEBUG / Conditional in DebugLog.cs).

ON THE UI-THREAD HOT PATH: confirmed _ui = DispatcherQueue.GetForCurrentThread() at ctor (EngineClient.cs:368) and Apply is dispatched via _ui.TryEnqueue(() => Apply(ev)) (line 705) — Apply genuinely runs on the UI thread. Apply emits two unconditional Info writes per event ([APPLY:N] enter line 949, exit line 1260). LastProgress = p.Progress (line 1032) is set at most 10 Hz (throttled, line 1029) and fires Set<T> PropertyChanged each time; 2 subscribers act on LastProgress (SidebarProcessingControl.xaml.cs:64, SidebarPipelineProgress.xaml.cs:179), each emitting one ENGINE-SUB Debug write. (Note: Phase = p.Progress.Phase at line 1036 does NOT fan out in steady state because Set<T> compares equality and Phase is unchanged during tagging — so the dominant driver is LastProgress + the two Apply lines, not Phase.)

QUANTIFIED COST: engine throttles Progress to 10 Hz (scan_session.rs:529 PROGRESS_THROTTLE_MS=100) + ~1.4 BatchSummary/s at 140 f/s (line 504). Per ProgressEvent = ~4 disk-write cycles (1 enter Info + 2 ENGINE-SUB Debug + 1 exit Info). At 10 Hz that is ~40/s, plus ~3/s from BatchSummary ≈ ~43 synchronous open/stat/stat/append/flush/close cycles per second on the UI thread under one global lock, sustained for the entire multi-minute scan. On the 4 GB DirectML target the disk is simultaneously contended by the engine's SQLite WAL writes, so each flushing AppendAllText can stall multiple ms and steal directly from grid layout/composition — the exact mid-scan stutter class this team already fixed elsewhere (windows/CLAUDE.md documents caching brushes to avoid 10 Hz UI-thread costs in SidebarPipelineProgress).

CORRECTIONS (do not change the verdict): (1) The finder overstated AppPaths.EnsureDirectories() — it has a run-once volatile guard (AppPaths.cs:72 `if (_directoriesEnsured) return;`), so it is one bool read after the first call, not 6 CreateDirectory syscalls. (2) The finder said ~30+ cycles/s; my derivation is ~43/s, so if anything the finder UNDERSTATED the rate. The two stat syscalls + open/flush/close per line under the global lock are real and unmitigated, and the truncation size check at lines 63-70 does re-stat on every call (its own comment says 'truncated at startup' but the check is placed per-line — confirming the finder's point).

FIX IS SAFE: the suggested fix (persistent StreamWriter or off-thread Channel/queue + flush on a background timer; move the >10MB size check off the per-call path) preserves every log line and the load-bearing [APPLY:N]/[ENGINE-SUB] forensic tracing that windows/CLAUDE.md explicitly says NOT to strip — it changes only the SINK, not what is logged. No correctness/quality regression. One implementer caveat: the forensic value depends on the last lines surviving a native fast-fail, so the off-thread sink must autoflush promptly (small interval) rather than buffer a long tail; a persistent-FileStream-with-per-line-flush variant removes the open/close+double-stat cost while keeping per-line durability. That is a design detail, not a blocker.

Confidence 0.85 (not higher) because the absolute per-write stall on the actual target disk is hardware-dependent and the visible-stutter magnitude needs an on-hardware A/B to fully confirm; but the code-level facts (UI-thread, no buffering, no level gate, ~43 flush-close/s under a global lock, safe sink-only fix) are unambiguous and meet the 'genuinely significant at scale AND safe fix' bar.

VERIFIED IN CODE. DebugLog.Write (DebugLog.cs:55-83) does, with NO level gating (Grep found no #if DEBUG / Conditional / level filter), per call: File.Exists + new FileInfo(path).Length (2 stat syscalls, lines 63-66) then File.AppendAllText (line 75 — opens/writes/flushes/CLOSES the handle every call) under one process-global s_writeLock (line 19, 73). EngineClient.Apply runs ON THE UI THREAD (EngineClient.cs:705 _ui.TryEnqueue(() => Apply(ev)); the stdout loop at 660-705 dispatches every decoded frame with no batching) and emits exactly 2 unconditional Info writes per event ([APPLY:N] enter at 949, exit at 1260).

The claim is correct on mechanism and is actually CONSERVATIVE on rate. It estimated ~12 events/s from the engine's 100ms throttle, but: (1) the engine 100ms throttle is a FLOOR (scan_session.rs:544 fires on elapsed>=100ms OR files_since>=1000, bursty), and EngineClient.cs:126-128 explicitly notes "on a fast scan that's 100+ events/s"; (2) the 10 Hz throttle the claim cites is the C# LastProgress REPAINT gate (EngineClient.cs:157,1029-1035) which gates PropertyChanged/ENGINE-SUB but NOT the Apply Info pair — those two writes fire at the FULL engine event rate at the top/bottom of Apply, before the gate. So the real UI-thread write rate is up to ~200 forced open+2stat+write+flush+close cycles/s (2 x ~100 events/s), not ~24.

MAGNITUDE: warm NTFS SSD ~50-150 us/AppendAllText cycle => ~200/s x ~100us ≈ 20 ms/s (~2% of one core) — modest on average. The real harm is TAIL LATENCY on the 4 GB / disk-contended target: with the engine's SQLite WAL hammering the same disk, individual synchronous open/flush/close calls stall multiple ms; a few 2-5 ms synchronous flushes/s landing on the UI thread between 16.6 ms composition frames drop frames and cause exactly the mid-scan stutter that the surrounding throttle machinery (PerfAudit-#8 at EngineClient.cs:126, the 10 Hz LastProgress gate, SidebarPipelineProgress brush caching per CLAUDE.md) was built to avoid. The off-UI-thread stderr writer (EngineClient.cs:728 DebugLog.Debug) contends the SAME global lock, adding lock-wait stalls onto the UI thread on top of disk stalls.

Two claim overstatements, both minor: (a) AppPaths.EnsureDirectories is NOT 7 syscalls/call — it is gated by _directoriesEnsured (AppPaths.cs:72) and is a single bool check after first call; (b) it described the rate as ~12 events/s (under-count, not over-count). Neither weakens the core finding.

FIX IS SAFE: persistent StreamWriter or off-thread Channel/queue + flush timer changes only sink mechanics and preserves every log line, satisfying the CLAUDE.md load-bearing "do not strip [APPLY:N]/[ENGINE-SUB]" constraint. One real obligation: WriteCrashDump (DebugLog.cs:128-137) reads app.log from disk for the last-50-lines forensic tail and the native-fast-fail diagnosis depends on the trailing [APPLY:N] enter-without-exit line being on disk, so a buffered sink MUST flush inside SafeRun's catch, WriteCrashDump, and MarkCleanExit. That is implementable and not a regression if done. Net: genuinely significant tail-latency cost on the target hardware + a safe, line-preserving fix => real. Confidence held below 0.9 because the average-case CPU cost is small and the frame-drop impact is a tail-latency argument that warrants an on-hardware A/B to size precisely.

VERIFIED IN CODE. DebugLog.Write (DebugLog.cs:55-83) does, per call with NO level gate (Info/Warn/Error/Debug all route to Write, lines 21-24): File.Exists(path) + new FileInfo(path).Length = two real stat syscalls (lines 63-66), then File.AppendAllText under lock(s_writeLock) (lines 73-75) = open+write+flush+close of a file handle every call, serialized on one process-global lock. (One claim overstatement: AppPaths.EnsureDirectories at line 59 is NOT two stat syscalls after startup — AppPaths.cs:68-72 made it a volatile-bool early-return precisely for this hot path. The stat cost is the File.Exists+FileInfo pair, not EnsureDirectories.)

The hot path is real and on the UI thread: _ui = DispatcherQueue.GetForCurrentThread() captured on the UI thread (EngineClient.cs:368); StdoutLoopAsync dispatches every decoded frame via _ui.TryEnqueue(() => Apply(ev)) (line 705). Apply unconditionally emits two Info writes per event — [APPLY:N] enter (line 949) and [APPLY:N] exit (line 1260) — and Set<T> fans PropertyChanged out synchronously (line 1301) to ~15+ subscribers, of which SidebarPipelineProgress logs a Debug [ENGINE-SUB] line on every LastProgress (line 179) BEFORE its SyncStage _lastRenderedIndex early-return, so that write is not throttled.

Event rate confirmed in engine: Progress throttled to 10 Hz (scan_session.rs:529 PROGRESS_THROTTLE_MS=100) plus per-batch BatchSummary ~1.4/s at 140 files/s (line 504) → ~12 events/s reach Apply for the whole multi-minute scan. Cost: ~12 events/s x (2 Apply Info + >=1 ENGINE-SUB Debug) = ~30-36 open/stat/append/close cycles per second on the UI thread under one global lock. On the 4 GB target the disk is simultaneously hit by the engine's SQLite WAL, so each open+flush can stall 1-several ms; ~30/s of those steal a real, recurring slice of the UI thread's layout/composition budget — the exact mid-scan stutter class the codebase already engineers around (10 Hz throttles, ctor-cached brushes per CLAUDE.md). This is a textbook synchronous-IO-on-UI-thread defect, significant at the 'tens of thousands of files' scale.

REGRESSION LENS — fix is SAFE: a persistent StreamWriter / Channel-buffered off-thread sink plus moving the >10 MB size check off the per-line path changes only sink mechanics. It touches no ML code, no clustering/tags/embeddings, no gated 6 GB-box behavior; logged bytes are identical, only when/how they reach disk changes. The one constraint — preserving [APPLY:N] enter/exit + [ENGINE-SUB] interleaving for fast-fail forensics (CLAUDE.md marks it load-bearing) — is explicitly honored by the suggested fix (it keeps the tracing, makes the sink cheap); an ordered Channel or a tight AutoFlush keeps forensic ordering, with a minor, mitigable risk of losing the final lines on a native fast-fail. No correctness/quality exposure. Confidence capped at 0.82 because the absolute per-write stall on the actual target disk (SSD vs paging-pressured/HDD) and the resulting visible stutter magnitude are owner-gated on-hardware quantities, not derivable from source.

confirmed.title: DBWriter computes redact_path_for_log() for every file on the persist hot path, only to feed error-context closures that almost never run
confirmed.description: Inside the per-file loop `for f in buffer.iter()` (line 240), every file eagerly computes `let rp = crate::platform::redact_path_for_log(&f.path);` (line 247). `rp` is used ONLY inside `.with_context(|| format!("... {rp}"))` closures (lines 271, 285, 319, 325, 336, 362, 384, 408, 411, 416, 419, 429, 432, 437, 440, 459, 465), all of which are invoked only when a SQL statement returns an error — i.e. effectively never on the success path. `redact_path_for_log` (platform.rs:13) is not cheap: it does `path.to_string_lossy().to_string()` (1 String alloc), `s.to_lowercase()` (2nd alloc), calls `crate::paths::root()`, and `root.to_string_lossy().to_lowercase()` (more allocs/work) on every call. This is the DBWriter persist path that drains every TaggedFile in the scan, so at tens of thousands of files it is one heavy redaction pass + multiple String allocations per file, all discarded unused on the common success path. The redaction itself is correct and must stay for the error case (it strips user paths off the IPC/log wire), but it should be computed lazily.
confirmed.trigger: Read dbwriter.rs flush() per-file loop in full; confirmed `rp` is consumed only by `with_context(|| format!(...))` closures (lazy, error-only), then read platform.rs redact_path_for_log to confirm it allocates 2+ Strings and calls paths::root() per invocation.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Delete the eager `let rp = ...` binding and inline the redaction inside each error closure, which only runs on failure: e.g. `.with_context(|| format!("insert+id for {}", crate::platform::redact_path_for_log(&f.path)))`. Output is byte-identical when an error actually occurs; the success path then does zero redaction work. (Alternatively keep one binding but make it a closure captured by reference.)

confirmed.title: Discovery's per-entry noise filter allocates a String for every walked file/dir even though the predicate takes &str
confirmed.description: In the jwalk `process_read_dir` `children.retain(...)` closure (lines 229-240) every directory entry computes `let name = entry.file_name().to_string_lossy().to_string();` (line 232) — an owned heap `String` — then passes `&name` to `is_noise_directory(&name)` / `is_noise_file(&name)`, both of which take `&str` (discovery.rs:362, 391). `to_string_lossy()` already returns a `Cow<str>` that derefs to `&str`, so the trailing `.to_string()` is a pure-churn allocation. This closure runs once for EVERY entry the parallel walk enumerates across the whole tree — every file and directory, including the large majority that are FileKind::Other and later discarded — so on a tens-of-thousands-to-millions-entry corpus it is one wasted heap allocation per entry on the discovery walk. (Severity medium rather than high because discovery is not the >=140 files/s throughput bottleneck — the file header and CLIP-batch comments confirm the GPU/RAM++ stage is — but it is a real, simple, scale-proportional allocation win.)
confirmed.trigger: Grep for to_string_lossy/to_string/clone in discovery.rs surfaced line 232; read the retain closure and both is_noise_* signatures to confirm `name` is only ever borrowed as &str and the .to_string() is unused as an owned value.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Drop the `.to_string()` and bind the Cow: `let name = entry.file_name().to_string_lossy();` then call `is_noise_directory(&name)` / `is_noise_file(&name)` (the &Cow<str> coerces to &str). Zero per-entry allocations on the common ASCII path; behavior is identical.

- Line 232: `let name = entry.file_name().to_string_lossy().to_string();` — `to_string_lossy()` returns `Cow<str>`; the trailing `.to_string()` forces an owned heap `String`. For valid-UTF-8 names (the common case) the Cow is `Borrowed`, so `.to_string()` is one pure-churn heap allocation+free; for invalid names it's a second copy.
- `name` is used ONLY at lines 234 (`!is_noise_directory(&name)`) and 236 (`!is_noise_file(&name)`) inside the retain closure (229-240). It is never moved, stored, or otherwise consumed as an owned value.
- Both predicates take `&str`: `is_noise_directory(name: &str)` (line 362) and `is_noise_file(name: &str)` (line 391). The suggested fix `let name = entry.file_name().to_string_lossy();` + `&name` coerces `&Cow<str>` -> `&str` cleanly. Behavior is byte-identical — SAFE, zero correctness/quality risk.
- Scale: the closure runs once per directory entry (every file AND dir) across the whole parallel jwalk, so on a tens-of-thousands-to-millions-entry corpus this is genuinely one wasted alloc per entry — a real, scale-proportional win.

confirmed.title: OCR clones the full decoded RGB frame instead of moving it; doubles per-file frame memory on PNG/screenshot-heavy libraries
confirmed.description: rgb is owned by the `if let Some((rgb, w, h)) = image_source` block (line 1324) and the OCR call at line 1674 (run_ocr_blocking(rgb.clone(), w, h).await) is its LAST use — nothing after the OCR branch (the block ends at line 1684; push_enriched_extras does not touch rgb) reads it. The clone() is therefore pure waste: it allocates and memcpys a second full decoded frame (typically ~6-24 MB RGB8, up to ~150 MB at the 50 MP MAX_DECODED_PIXELS cap) for the duration of the spawn_blocking OCR call. should_run_ocr returns true for EVERY PNG without an EXIF camera model (tagging.rs:1752-1755) plus any file named screenshot/scan/receipt/etc., so on screenshot/document-heavy libraries OCR runs on a large fraction of files. On the MemoryTier::Low 4 GB target each concurrent OCR file then pins 2x its frame instead of 1x, raising peak RSS and OS paging pressure exactly where the working set is already the largest live allocation (per scan.rs:266-275). The clone also costs a full-frame memcpy on the worker before it hands off to OCR.
confirmed.trigger: Scan a library with many PNGs/screenshots on the low-RAM box: every OCR-eligible file allocates a redundant full-frame copy that lives for the whole spawn_blocking OCR call.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Move rgb into OCR instead of cloning: `run_ocr_blocking(rgb, w, h).await` (the destructured rgb is owned and unused afterward, so the borrow checker accepts the move). This removes one full-frame allocation + memcpy per OCR file and halves the OCR-file frame residency.

verdicts.reasoning:

CORE MECHANICS CONFIRMED: rgb is destructured at tagging.rs:1324 (`if let Some((rgb, w, h)) = image_source`); the OCR call at :1674 `run_ocr_blocking(rgb.clone(), w, h)` is rgb's genuine LAST use — the `if let` block closes at :1684 (only stats/perf lines 1680-1682 follow), and push_enriched_extras at :1691 is outside the block and touches only `tagged`, never rgb. run_ocr_blocking (:1857) takes `rgb: Vec<u8>` by value and moves it into spawn_blocking (:1858 `move ||`) where recognize(&rgb,...) borrows it, so passing by move is functionally identical and the .clone() is pure waste. The suggested fix `run_ocr_blocking(rgb, w, h)` is a trivial, byte-identical, borrow-checker-accepted move with ZERO correctness/quality risk.

confirmed.title: FilePreviewSheet 1024px preview decodes at the source's native resolution (no DecodePixelWidth cap)
confirmed.description: The preview path builds `var bmp = new BitmapImage(); await bmp.SetSourceAsync(captured);` with NO DecodePixelWidth set. The `captured` source is the StorageItemThumbnail from `GetThumbnailAsync(ThumbnailMode.SingleItem, 1024, UseCurrentScale)`. The shell's `requestedSize` is a *minimum/desired* hint, not a hard cap: for image kinds the IThumbnailProvider chain can hand back a thumbnail at or above 1024px (e.g. a large embedded JPEG thumbnail, or a provider that renders near native resolution), and a BitmapImage with no DecodePixelWidth decodes the returned stream at its full native dimensions. The class comment at lines 319-324 asserts a fixed "~4 MB decode" (1024 x 1024 x 4), but that figure only holds if the shell returns exactly 1024px; an over-returned 2-3K-edge surface decodes to 16-36 MB each. With PreviewCacheCap=4 retained MRU entries that is up to ~64-144 MB of decoded RGBA pinned in the preview cache on a 4 GB box, versus the ~16 MB the design assumes. This is the ONLY decode path in this file set that does not cap: PeopleViewModel face thumbs cap at DecodePixelWidth 120/80 and ThumbnailService caps at 192 (ThumbnailService.cs:398,690); the 1024 preview is the outlier. Not a hot loop (one image at a time, cap 4), so severity is low, but it is a real, free bound to add.
confirmed.trigger: Open the preview sheet (or arrow-key through siblings) on image files whose shell IThumbnailProvider returns a thumbnail larger than 1024px on the requested edge (common for photos with large embedded thumbnails or RAW/HEIC providers that render near native res).
confirmed.suggested_fix: Set the decode target explicitly to match the requested edge: `var bmp = new BitmapImage { DecodePixelWidth = 1024 };` before `await bmp.SetSourceAsync(captured);` (line 359). This caps the decoded surface at the displayed size regardless of what the shell hands back, making the preview cache's 4-entry budget the ~16 MB the comment already documents. Same one-line hardening the other three decode sites already apply.

contested.title: Authenticode revocation check + pre-spawn SHA-256 hashing run synchronously on the UI thread before the first frame
contested.description: App.OnLaunched calls EngineClient.Instance.StartAsync() directly on the UI thread (App.xaml.cs:87) BEFORE `new MainWindow()` (App.xaml.cs:183). StartAsync is `async Task`, but its first real `await` is `await SendCommandAsync(...)` at EngineClient.cs:563 — everything before that runs SYNCHRONOUSLY on the UI thread. That synchronous prefix includes WinVerifyTrustChecker.Verify(enginePath) at line 428, which is configured with fdwRevocationChecks = WTD_REVOKE_WHOLECHAIN and dwProvFlags = WTD_REVOCATION_CHECK_CHAIN (WinVerifyTrustChecker.cs:84,90). On a signed RELEASE build (the ship target) WinVerifyTrust then performs a full CRL/OCSP revocation lookup over the network for every cert in the chain, synchronously, on the UI thread. On a cold/slow/captive network or behind a filtering proxy this blocks for seconds (CryptoAPI default per-URL timeout is ~15 s) with no window on screen yet — the classic 'app icon bounces then nothing' cold-start stall. On release builds with FILEID_EV_THUMBPRINT set, the same synchronous prefix ALSO reads and SHA-256-hashes the multi-MB LTO engine .exe twice (lines 472-475 and 492-494) on the UI thread (TOCTOU mitigation) before Process.Start. Net: the entire integrity gate (network-bound revocation + two full-file reads of a large binary) is on the first-frame critical path. This is a 4 GB box where disk is already the bottleneck; serializing two full reads of the engine binary plus a network round-trip ahead of first paint is a real, user-visible cold-start cliff.
contested.trigger: Cold start of a signed release build; pronounced on a slow or offline network (revocation lookup timeout) and on a cold disk (two full reads of the large engine .exe).
contested.suggested_fix: Move the synchronous prefix of StartAsync (signature Verify + the pre-spawn SHA-256 hashing) off the UI thread: wrap the verify+hash+Process.Start block in a single Task.Run, or restructure StartAsync so an `await Task.Run(...)` (or `await Task.Yield()`) executes before WinVerifyTrustChecker.Verify so control returns to OnLaunched and the window is created/activated while integrity runs on a background thread. Independently, consider relaxing revocation to WTD_REVOKE_NONE or WTD_CACHE_ONLY_URL_RETRIEVAL for the spawn-time gate (the TOCTOU hash already covers tamper) so the network round-trip can't gate launch.

verdicts.reasoning:
Structural claims all verified against the code:

1. App.xaml.cs:87 calls `EngineClient.Instance.StartAsync()` fire-and-forget (assigned to EngineStartedTask, NOT awaited) on the UI thread, BEFORE `new MainWindow()` (line 183) and `window.Activate()` (line 186). Window creation does not depend on the engine spawn result.

2. EngineClient.cs:381 `StartAsync` is `async Task` but its first incomplete `await` is `await SendCommandAsync(...)` at line 563. There is NO await between method entry and line 563 — the `Task.Run` calls at 544-545 are fire-and-forget, not awaited. So C# async-state-machine semantics mean the entire prefix runs SYNCHRONOUSLY on the UI thread before control returns to OnLaunched: WinVerifyTrustChecker.Verify (428), pre-spawn SHA-256 (472-474), re-hash (492-494), Process.Start (537). The finder's "everything before 563 is synchronous on the UI thread" is correct.

3. WinVerifyTrustChecker.cs:84 sets fdwRevocationChecks = WTD_REVOKE_WHOLECHAIN and :90 dwProvFlags = WTD_REVOCATION_CHECK_CHAIN — on a SIGNED build this triggers synchronous CRL/OCSP network lookups for every cert in the chain. CryptoAPI's default per-URL revocation timeout is ~15s, so on an offline/captive/proxied network this blocks the UI thread for seconds before any window paints — the classic "icon bounces, nothing appears" stall. (An UNSIGNED binary short-circuits to TRUST_E_NOSIGNATURE with no network call, so this bites only signed builds.)

4. The two SHA-256 passes only run when FILEID_EV_THUMBPRINT is present in the process env (lines 468, 488) — the configured signed-release install.

Cost re-derivation (correcting the finder's overstatement): the engine binary is 9.5 MB (measured), not "multi-MB" in an alarming sense. Two SHA-256 passes = ~19 MB hashed (~10-20 ms CPU at ~1-2 GB/s); the second read is almost always OS-cache-warm (file read ms earlier), so the real I/O is one cold ~9.5 MB read (~20-150 ms on slow disk, negligible on SSD). So hashing is modest (~20-150 ms), NOT the dominant cost. The dominant cost is the synchronous revocation lookup: sub-second on a healthy online network (still a real few-hundred-ms pre-paint block once you add hashing), multi-second to ~15s+ on the offline/captive/slow-revocation tail.

Why REAL despite the finder overstating the hashing: this IS on the cold-start critical path before first paint for the ship target (signed release, which is the documented intended config — EV cert is the sole external blocker per NEXT.md/SHIP.md). The suggested fix is genuinely SAFE with zero correctness/quality regression: window creation (MainWindow/Activate) is fully independent of the engine spawn — the app already tolerates a not-yet-ready engine via the engine pill and WaitForReadyAsync. Wrapping Verify+hash+Process.Start in Task.Run (or `await Task.Yield()` before Verify) lets the window paint immediately while integrity runs on a background thread; the integrity gate's verdict still gates the spawn exactly as before, so the TOCTOU/tamper protection is unchanged.

Caveats lowering confidence below certainty: (a) on current dev/unsigned builds the cost is near-zero (no thumbprint → no hashing; unsigned → no revocation network call), so this is a pre-ship issue that only bites the future signed build; (b) on a healthy online network the typical-case cost is a few hundred ms rather than the dramatic multi-second cliff, so the finder's "high / seconds" framing applies mainly to the network-degraded tail. Net: a legitimate cold-start latency issue on the ship-target critical path with a safe, standard fix — verdict real.

The structural observation is accurate but the magnitude/severity is substantially overstated, and the scariest sub-claim is dead code in every build that exists today.

WHAT IS TRUE:
- StartAsync (EngineClient.cs:381) runs synchronously until its first await at line 563; OnLaunched (App.xaml.cs:87) calls it directly on the UI thread BEFORE `new MainWindow()` (App.xaml.cs:183). So WinVerifyTrustChecker.Verify (line 428) does run synchronously on the UI thread before first paint. Confirmed.
- Verify always uses WTD_REVOKE_WHOLECHAIN (WinVerifyTrustChecker.cs:84) + WTD_REVOCATION_CHECK_CHAIN (line 90) regardless of thumbprint. On a signed binary this can trigger CRL/OCSP. Confirmed.

WHY THE COST IS NOT SIGNIFICANT AT SCALE:
1. The "two full SHA-256 reads of the engine .exe on the UI thread" — the claim's headline cost — is gated on `!string.IsNullOrEmpty(expectedThumb)` (EngineClient.cs:468, 488). `expectedThumb` comes ONLY from `Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable("FILEID_EV_THUMBPRINT")` (line 427). That variable is a BUILD-TIME/CI signing var (release.yml:58,80) consumed only by signtool. The MSI (Product.wxs) has NO `<Environment>` element, and there is NO `PROD_EV_THUMBPRINT` constant baked into WinVerifyTrustChecker.cs — NEXT.md:116 lists pinning it as a FUTURE post-cert TODO. So at runtime on any shipped/dev build today, expectedThumb is null and the entire double-hash block is INERT. Cost today: 0 ms.
2. Even in the future pinned-thumbprint case: binary is ~9.5 MB (release FileIDEngine.exe = 9,492,992 bytes, not "multi-MB large"). 2x SHA-256 ≈ 10-20 ms CPU; second read is warm-cache; realistic added latency tens-to-low-hundreds of ms only on a genuinely cold disk. One-time, fixed.
3. The revocation network stall is the only multi-second component, but requires (a) a SIGNED release — which does not exist yet (the EV cert is the sole unshipped blocker per SHIP.md/NEXT.md), (b) a degraded/offline/captive network, and (c) is OS-CRL/OCSP-cached per validity period; the signed FileID.exe itself already triggers loader/SmartScreen chain validation, warming the cache.
4. Nothing here scales with the tens-of-thousands-of-files / 4 GB lens. It is a fixed one-time startup cost, dwarfed by WinUI/Mica/XAML init, .NET JIT, and engine spawn + ONNX session init (CLIP/RAM++ load is seconds). It does not scale with corpus size at all.

FIX RISK: The Task.Run/Task.Yield half is cheap and safe, but the suggested revocation relaxation (WTD_REVOKE_NONE / CACHE_ONLY) reverses the deliberate SEC-4 hardening (WinVerifyTrustChecker.cs:58-63) and is a security-posture regression.

Net: real code smell, negligible at-scale cost, headline sub-claim is inert in current builds, worst case is future/conditional. Refuted on magnitude/severity. Relevant files: platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/ViewModels/EngineClient.cs (381-578), platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/App.xaml.cs (44-216), platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/Services/WinVerifyTrustChecker.cs, platforms/windows/installer/FileID.Msi/Product.wxs, .github/workflows/release.yml.

THREADING + FLAGS CONFIRMED, COST/FIX NOT. App.OnLaunched (sync void, UI thread) calls StartAsync() un-awaited at App.xaml.cs:87; StartAsync's first real await is `await SendCommandAsync` at EngineClient.cs:563, so the prefix — CAS gate, WinVerifyTrustChecker.Verify (428), two SHA-256 reads (472-474, 492-494), Process.Start (537) — runs synchronously on the UI thread before control returns to line 88 and before new MainWindow()/Activate() (183/186). WinVerifyTrustChecker.cs:84/90 do set WTD_REVOKE_WHOLECHAIN + WTD_REVOCATION_CHECK_CHAIN. So the structural claim (integrity gate on the first-frame critical path) is accurate.

But the magnitude is overstated and the fix is unsafe, so it fails the skeptical real bar:

1) Both expensive paths are dormant in the SHIPPED-TODAY state. The double-SHA-256 path only runs when FILEID_EV_THUMBPRINT is non-empty (EngineClient.cs:468/488), and that env var is set NOWHERE — not in installer/FileID.Msi/Product.wxs, build scripts, or app code; it is only ever read (EngineClient.cs:427). So the two full-file reads are currently dead. The network-revocation stall requires an actually EV-signed binary, but Windows CLAUDE.md lists Authenticode EV signing as "in progress / not done", so the current ship binary is unsigned: Verify returns Unsigned via TRUST_E_NOSIGNATURE with NO network round-trip (no chain to revoke-check). The headline "blocks for seconds before first frame" is a conditional future-ship-state risk, not present behavior.

2) Even when active, the hash cost is modest: engine is 9,492,992 bytes (~9.05 MB). Two reads ≈ ~10 ms warm, ~150 ms cold-HDD (~120 MB/s), ~300 ms slow 4 GB-box disk — meaningful but not a "cliff." Only the offline/captive-network revocation timeout (~15 s CryptoAPI per-URL) is severe, and only once signed.

3) FIX IS CORRECTNESS-RISKY (regression-risk lens). The Set<T> helper (EngineClient.cs:1294-1302) fires PropertyChanged SYNCHRONOUSLY on the calling thread — it does not marshal to the UI thread. The prefix writes State = Starting (414) and multiple State = Crashed (433/439/449/479/498/506/555). The respawn path deliberately wraps StartAsync in _ui.TryEnqueue (line 820) so these setters fire on the UI thread for live view subscribers. The suggested "wrap verify+hash+Process.Start in Task.Run" would move those State PropertyChanged invocations onto a thread-pool thread while view subscribers (SidebarEngineStatus, SidebarQueueList, etc.) are attached during a respawn — reintroducing the native-fast-fail class the Windows CLAUDE.md documents three times (V15.2/V15.2.1/V15.4). A safe fix must also re-dispatch every State write (or change Set to marshal), which is a non-trivial restructure, not the drop-in proposed. (The independent revocation-relaxation half — WTD_CACHE_ONLY_URL_RETRIEVAL — is benign and reasonable, but it doesn't address the framed UI-thread-blocking claim and the TOCTOU hash being relaxed alongside it touches the security gate.) No ML/clustering/embedding output is affected either way, but the UI-thread-affinity regression is a real stability hazard. Net: structural observation real, current cost negligible, worst case conditional+future, and the proposed fix fails the safety bar → refuted.
